[teacher] More, more. Basically a decimal is a number that's based on the number 10. Actually, all of our numbers are decimals because our number system is based on 10. Even the word decimal comes from the Latin word for 10, decum. When people talk about decimals, they're talking about numbers that are only parts of a whole. Imagine you've got a rectangle that represents one whole object. If you divide it into 10, smaller, equal- sized rectangles, you now have 10 parts of 1, tenths. If you divide each of those into 10 smaller rectangles, you then have 10 times 10 or 100 parts of 1, hundredths. Then if you divide those into 10 smaller rectangles, you have 10 times 100, or 1,000 parts of 1, thousandths. All of these fractions, tenths, hundredths, and thousandths, are called decimal units. Writing a decimal is pretty easy. In our number system, I can count from 0 to 9 in a place. Each place increases by the power of 10. Because decimal units are smaller than one unit, you actually move to the right of the decimal place. To do that, meet this guy, the decimal point. Numbers to the left of the decimal point are whole numbers, and numbers to the right are decimals. Except for the units, every place on the left side has a decimal equivalent on the right, tens and tenths, hundreds and hundredths, thousandths and thousandths. This number is thirty-nine and eighty-three hundredths. Actually, decimals are really important. We use them everyday to talk about money. Right, one cent is just one hundredth of a dollar. We also use decimals for weight, measuring distances, baseball statistics, temperatures, and even stock prices. Do you need more reasons? Well, arithmetic is a lot easier with decimals than it is with fractions. As long as you line the decimal points up, you can add and subtract them just like whole numbers. Also, decimal numbers are easier to compare. By adding zeros to the right of the shorter number to make the numbers the same length, you can tell right away which one is bigger. Zero is just a place holder. Adding zeros to the right of the decimal doesn't change the value of the number. Some numbers do go on adding zeros to the right forever. For example, the decimal equivalent of the fraction one third starts out as .333, and just keeps going on and on. These decimals are called repeating decimals. You could round that number off to three tenths, or thirty- three hundredths, but it's more accurate to put a line over the last number. That line indicates that the number keeps on going. Decimals that end are called terminating decimals, but some decimals, called irrational numbers, don't end or repeat. One example is pi, which is the ratio or circumference of circle to its diameter. Pi starts off 3.14159 – then it just keeps going for so long that scientists have found millions of its decimals without any repeating pattern. Convinced yet? One hundredth of a second. See? Decimals matter.
